lines.text: The bell hasn't rung yet, but half the room is already staring at the project sheet taped to the whiteboard.
All right. Settle down before the bell does it for you.
lines.action: taps two fingers on his desk

lines.text: Semester project. Fifteen percent of your grade, presented to the whole grade in front of a panel. So — no, this is not the one you copy off a website the night before.
It's giving free period energy already, not gonna lie.
lines.action: leans chair back on two legs

lines.text: Noted, Mr. Bellamy. Groups are groups — nobody gets to pick their favorite people. That's not what this is for.
Please not Dez, please not Dez—
lines.action: pen tapping fast against her notebook

lines.text: Osei, Bellamy, Chen, Alvarez. You're group four.
Yesss, okay, we got this.
Okay. Okay, that's — fine. That's fine.

lines.action: glances at the player for half a second — unreadable — then looks back down at his desk

lines.text: He's the kid from the lunchroom. First day. I still remember the sound of that tray hitting the floor.
Okay. Group four. No pressure.
Library study room, three days later. Dez has his chair tipped back, phone in hand. Priya already has color-coded tabs in her binder.
Okay so — real talk — my sister did this exact project last year. Like, exact. Same teacher, same everything.
Same rubric?
Basically. Pretty sure I still got the files somewhere.
Relax, it's not that deep. We tweak some words, boom, done, we go get food.
That's — I mean, technically if we cite—
No, that's not — okay.

lines.action: says nothing, pencil gone still, watching

lines.text: What do you think? You're quiet over there.
Here we go.
It's not that deep. We're not, like, committing a crime.
I mean... she's kind of right, though. If it's basically copied, that's not nothing.
Fine. Fine! We do it fresh. Happy?

lines.action: glances at player, something shifts in his posture, barely a nod

lines.text: I'm never happy, it's part of my charm.
Okay. Okay, let's just — divide sections. That I can do.
Yeah. Okay.
Say less.
lines.action: already pulling up files on his phone

lines.text: Sister's a legend, honestly.
So we're... using it. As is? Or—
As inspiration. Heavily inspired.
Right. Cool. Great.
lines.emotion: doesn't sound like she means it

lines.action: says nothing, looks down, something closes off in his face

lines.text: Easier. That's all this is. Easier.
See, this is why you're my favorite group member.
Structure only, huh.
Fine, whatever's faster.
Okay. Yeah. That actually — that works.
That's the first thing today that made sense.
Wow, tough crowd.
So we split it — sections, deadlines, the whole boring part.
Already on it. I have a spreadsheet. Don't judge me.
No judgment. I have seventeen tabs open right now just for my part.
Priya's kitchen table, days later. Printouts everywhere, half a bag of pretzels between them. Dez has migrated to his phone. Priya's redoing citations for the third time.
Author, date, page — no wait, that's not—
You good?
I'm redoing your citations. For the second time.
That's what I like to hear.
That was not a compliment.
You've been weirdly quiet. You good?
Yeah. I mean—
It's fine. I'll figure it out.
Figure out what?
The part I'm supposed to present. I don't — really get it.
I've just been writing stuff down that sounds right.
Wait, since when?
Since the beginning, I mean. I didn't want to be the guy who didn't get it.
Okay, walk me through what you've got so far.
It's this part. The, uh — the cause and effect thing. I don't know why it matters.
Okay, so — forget the textbook words for a sec. What actually happened?
I mean... this thing happened, and then this other thing happened because of it, and — oh.
Right! That's literally it. You just said the whole thing without the fancy words.
Huh.
Guess I don't totally suck at this.
Wish someone would walk me through my part like that.
lines.action: glances up from her own pile, quiet smile

lines.text: You draw robots too or is that just a me thing.
Robots, no. Extremely aggressive doodles of my own handwriting when I'm stressed, yes.
Okay. Yeah. That works.
lines.action: copies down the summary, doesn't argue

lines.text: See, easy. Nobody's gonna notice.
Yeah.
I'll just... read it off the card, I guess.
That was easy. Why does it feel like it wasn't.
Honestly? Just ask Mr. Okafor tomorrow before class. He'll explain it way better than I could.
Yeah. Sure.
I mean — it's fine. I'll figure it out.
You sure?
Yeah.

lines.action: glances between them, says nothing, goes back to her spreadsheet
doodling harder, doesn't bring it up again

lines.text: The night before. Priya's sent Dez's slides to the group chat with a single message.
can everyone review by 9?
Wait. I've read this sentence before. Word for word.
dez did you write this or...?
yeah why
this whole paragraph is from that history site. barely even reworded.
it's basically the same info, who cares. we're not gonna get caught.
wait what
OH NO
relax. it's not that deep.
Video call, past ten. Dez's camera shows more ceiling than face.
Dez. Look — I get it, deadlines are brutal. But we can't present that tomorrow.
Yeah, no, I got it — I got it.
You don't, though. That's kind of the problem.
...It's not that I didn't want to. I just didn't get it. Like, actually didn't get it. And it was faster to just find something that sounded smart.
Okay, well — I have three hours before my mom makes me turn off my laptop. Let's use them.
I brought snacks. Emotionally. I have no actual snacks.
...Thanks. For real.
Okay. Section by section. What do you actually understand?
Fine. Let's just— let's do this.
They stay up far too late, rebuilding it piece by piece — messy, tired, theirs.
Hallway, before first bell. Mr. Okafor's unlocking his classroom door.
Mr. Okafor? Can I talk to you for a sec?
Charlotte. That's usually not a good sign this early.
lines.action: turns, taps two fingers on the doorframe

lines.text: I mean, maybe it's just me, but — some of Dez's slides are basically copied from a website. Not really reworded.
I appreciate you telling me. That's not nothing, coming here before class.
I didn't want to just... let it happen. But I also didn't want it to be a whole thing.
It's already a whole thing. You just made it a fixable one.
I'll talk to him. You did the right call.
Is he gonna hate me?
Probably for about a week. Then he'll get over it. Mr. Bellamy's resilient, if nothing else.
it's late. let's just... hope for the best.
are you sure—
No response. The messages just sit there, delivered, unread.
The slides stay exactly as they are.
Maybe it'll be fine. Maybe.
The auditorium, full lights, a panel of teachers at a folding table up front. Group four is up.
...and that's basically how the trade routes shaped the whole region.
Interesting. Can you walk us through why that specific route mattered more than the others?
lines.action: leaning forward from the panel table
taps pen against his leg, faster than usual

lines.text: Silence stretches half a second too long.
That's actually the part we're least confident about — but here's what we do understand: it connected the coastal markets to the inland ones faster than the others.
And what would you need to solidify the rest?
More time with the primary sources, honestly. We leaned on secondary summaries for that section.
The panel exchanges a look — not disappointed. Something closer to impressed.
Good answer. Knowing what you don't know is half the assignment.
...Yeah. What she said.

lines.action: glances at player, something like respect on his face

lines.text: It's mainly the trade goods, and also, um, the geography basically forced it, so—
A teacher's eyebrow lifts. A beat passes. Then the panel moves on to the next question.
The moment passes. It doesn't feel like it passed clean.
We got away with it. So why does it feel like we didn't.
Grades go up outside the classroom door. Group four did fine — the number depends on what came before.
Hey. Uh — got a sec?
lines.action: catches up to player at the lockers, hesitant

lines.text: Yeah, of course.
I mean — the whole project. It was...
A lot.
I just wanted to say something about it. Actually say it, not just, like, think it.
The hallway thins out, lockers clanging shut down the row.
Hey.
Hey.
So. That was a whole thing, huh.
Yeah. Little bit.
Just a little.
The hallway thins out, lockers clanging shut down the row.
It wasn't perfect. Some stuff I'd do differently.
At least you're saying it.
Yeah. That's more than most people manage.
The hallway thins out, lockers clanging shut down the row.
Honestly? We got a good grade. I'll take it.
Yeah. I guess.
Mo walks off without saying much else.

options.text: I mean, maybe it's just me, but — I think we should just do our own work.
Whatever's easier. Not gonna fight about it.
What if we use hers for structure, but do the actual research ourselves?

a.text: I mean, maybe it's just me, but — I think we should just do our own thing.

b.text: Whatever's easier. Not gonna fight about it.
That's what I like to hear.

c.text: What if we use hers for like, structure — but do the actual research ourselves?

options.text: Show me what you've got. We'll go through it together.
Easiest thing — just copy the summary from the textbook.
Just ask Mr. Okafor tomorrow, he'll walk you through it.

a.text: Okay. Show me what you've got, we'll go through it together.

b.text: Easiest thing, just copy the summary from the textbook. Nobody's gonna dig that deep.

c.text: Just ask Mr. Okafor tomorrow, he'll walk you through it.

options.text: Push Dez one-on-one to redo it — with the group's help.
Tell Mr. Okafor before class, honestly.
Let it go for tonight. Hope it's fine.

a.text: dez. one on one. call me.

b.text: This isn't something a group chat fixes.
I'm telling Mr. Okafor before class tomorrow.

c.text: it's late. let's just... hope for the best.

options.text: Step in and admit the gap — explain what we do understand.
Let Dez bluff through it. Say nothing.

a.text: Honestly, that's the part we're still solidifying — but here's what we do know—

b.text: It's, uh, mainly because of the trade goods available along that route specifically—

options.text: Some of it almost went really wrong. Doing it right mattered more than doing it easy.
I mean, we got a good grade. That's what matters, right?
Honestly, it wasn't perfect. There's stuff I'd do differently.

a.text: Honestly? Some of it almost went really wrong. But I think doing it right mattered more than doing it easy.

b.text: I mean, we got a good grade. That's what matters, right?

c.text: Honestly, it wasn't perfect. There's stuff I'd do differently.

lines.text: You didn't have to tell Okafor about the slides. That could've gone a lot worse for you, socially.
Yeah, well. Felt worse to not say it.
Dez still isn't really looking at you, huh.
He'll get over it. Probably.
Yeah. I noticed.
Next semester. You're in this one now.
lines.action: adds player to a new group chat, no fanfare

lines.text: Didn't realize I was auditioning.
You weren't. You just passed.
You said something today. At the end there. About it almost going wrong.
Yeah. I mean — it did almost go wrong. The whole not-saying-anything thing. That was me too, for a while.
...Yeah. It was.
I noticed you didn't say anything back there. That's kind of your whole thing, huh.
Guess today you did, though.
Better late.
Working on it.
We actually fixed most of it ourselves. Before it got to Okafor-level bad.
Yeah, that middle-ground thing actually worked, somehow.
Don't sound so surprised. I have a spreadsheet for a reason.
Yeah. I noticed. The spreadsheet, I mean. Also the other thing.
Next semester. Same crew?
Same crew.
You told Okafor. Then still said it wasn't perfect. That's kind of a lot of honesty for one week.
Guess I used it all up.
Save some for next semester.
You let some stuff slide, though. Earlier. The slides thing. The ask-Okafor thing.
Yeah. That's part of what I'd do differently. Speak up sooner instead of after it's basically over.
...Yeah. That'd help.
Still counts. The saying-it part.
We found the middle a lot. Structure thing with Dez. Fixing his slides ourselves.
Yeah, but I don't think 'middle ground' is a fix for everything. Some stuff still needed a harder line.
Noted. For next time.
lines.action: writing it down, only half joking

lines.text: You're gonna make a spreadsheet too, aren't you.
Cool. Yeah. Cool.
That wasn't a real answer. Neither was mine.
You gonna let him just walk off like that?
You told Okafor. You spoke up at the panel. And now... this?
I mean — it's just a conversation, Mo. Grade's the grade.
Yeah. Sure.
That landed wrong. I could feel it land wrong.
We actually worked through most of it. Together. Feels weird to just... not talk about that.
I mean, it worked out. That's the main thing, right?
Sure. If you say so.

voiceNotes: Charlie deflects with humor ('part of my charm') and goes short/quiet rather than confrontational even when she's the one pushing back — her honesty scenes (3a, 7b, 9a) should still sound reluctant, not righteous. Dez's confidence is a shield: his lines get shorter and more clipped the closer he gets to being cornered (compare his fast 'relax, it's not that deep' in the group chat to his one-word 'fine' when called directly). Priya's anxiety shows in run-on sentences that she cuts herself off mid-thought when she catches herself being too honest too fast. Mo is the emotional barometer of the group — his silence (stage directions with no line) is doing as much work as his dialogue, and his one-liners ('yeah. I noticed.') should feel like they cost him something to say. Mr. Okafor never over-explains; his emotional beats live in the pauses before he responds, not in what he says.

alternatives: So, this is awkward, but — some of Dez's slides are basically word-for-word from a website.
I don't want to make this a bigger deal than it has to be, but I think you should look at Dez's slides before today.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original keeps Charlie's signature hedge ('maybe it's just me, but—'); these alternates trade her voice for more generic phrasing, useful if that verbal tic reads as repetitive across the episode.

alternatives: That's honestly the part we're still shaky on — but here's what we do know.
We're not a hundred percent on that yet. Here's what we can say for sure though.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original is slightly more formal to sell 'impressing the panel'; alternates lean more casual/13-year-old if the scene needs to feel less rehearsed.

alternatives: I mean — it almost fell apart. More than once. I think that's kind of the point, though.
Some of it was actually bad, not gonna lie. But I think we fixed it for the right reasons.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original is clean and a little too polished for Charlie's usual hedging style; alternates reintroduce her trademark 'I mean' softening.
